mappings.rationale: Disease detection, diagnosis, classification, and population-level study of disease determinants are central to outline A and B.
Traces the historical development of diagnosis, hospital medicine, and therapeutic techniques (B, C).
Directly addresses infectious and contagious diseases including cholera, influenza, dengue, MRSA, and vaccination (D.5).
Viral pathogens including coronavirus and SARS-CoV-2, along with vaccine technology, are relevant to contagious diseases (D.5).
Bacterial diseases, antibiotic resistance, tuberculosis, and leprosy as infectious agents connect to outline D.5.
Fungal pathogens causing skin diseases, respiratory infections, and parasitic infections are relevant to D.5, F.3.a, and F.8.g.
The full range of microbial agents -- bacteria, archaea, fungi, algae -- responsible for infectious diseases and their diagnosis connects to D.5.
Major epidemic infectious diseases -- influenza, malaria, plague, smallpox, tuberculosis, and COVID-19 -- are central to outline D.5.
The Black Death and coronavirus epidemics represent major contagious diseases discussed in D.5.
A major sexually transmitted disease, its diagnosis, treatment, and public health impact connect to D.5 and F.6.b.
Directly addresses tumours and cancers, including diagnosis, treatment options, chemotherapy, and drug development (E).
Immune disorders, allergenic diseases, and biological therapy via immune-based treatments are relevant to C.2.b and D.11.
Pharmacodynamic therapy, drug mechanisms, prescription drugs, and substance-related disease connect to C.2.c and D.10.
Blood diseases, haemoglobin disorders, and transfusion are relevant to diseases of the blood and blood-forming tissues (F.1.d).
Pituitary, thyroid, adrenal, and pancreatic disorders are central to endocrine system diseases (F.5).
Neurological conditions involving the basal ganglia and cerebral cortex are relevant to central nervous system diseases (F.9.d).
Colour blindness, retinal disorders, and vision impairment are relevant to disorders of the eye (F.9.f).
Anaesthesia is essential to surgical treatment, including opioid analgesia and blood pressure management (C.2.a).
Nutritional diseases from deficiencies and excesses -- including obesity and undernutrition -- are central to outline D.3.
Therapeutic potential for diabetes and macular degeneration is relevant to substitution therapy and organ transplants (C.2.e, C.2.k).
Mental illness, psychoanalysis, and psychiatric treatment are relevant to nervous system disorders and psychological therapy (F.9, C.2.l).
A major psychiatric disorder of the central nervous system, including brain imaging and drug treatment (F.9.d).
Depressive and bipolar disorders are classified among disorders of the nervous system (F.9.e).
Generalized anxiety disorder, heritability, and cognitive/behavioural treatments connect to nervous system disorders (F.9).
Degenerative disorders of the central nervous system, including diagnosis and treatment approaches, are central to F.9.d.
A neurodevelopmental disorder relevant to diseases present at birth and childhood diseases (D.6, D.7).
A developmental disorder affecting reading and language, relevant to disorders of speech and cognition (F.9.i).
Substance use disorders connect to poisoning (D.10) and neurological aspects of drug dependence (F.9).
Directly addresses psychological therapy as a major therapeutic technique (C.2.l).
A specific form of psychological therapy used to treat anxiety, depression, and other mental disorders (C.2.l).
Consent and euthanasia raise ethical considerations in formulating therapeutic regimens (C.1).
Consent, negligence, organ donation, and body parts are relevant to legal aspects of treatment and transplants (C.1, C.2.k).
Ionizing radiation is relevant to radiation therapy (C.2.f) and disorders caused by radiation exposure (D.8).
The microbiome's role in neurological disorders and nutrition connects to digestive system diseases (F.4).
Age-related degenerative conditions across body systems and gerontological aspects of disease connect to F.9.d.
